title: Merck — Oncology — Thesis
html:
Merck Oncology Thesis Tracker — Vinoth's POV

TA: Keytruda (pembrolizumab) / Broad Oncology IO Initiated: 2026-04-24 Last reviewed: 2026-04-24 Review cadence: Quarterly (next: July 2026)

How to use this document

Each thesis is a forward-looking bet — not a fact summary. The value is in being wrong and knowing why you were wrong. Update quarterly:

  • Did new evidence strengthen or weaken the thesis?
  • Should the thesis be revised, retired, or replaced?
  • What would you tell a Merck stakeholder if they asked your view on this?

Thesis 1: QLEX Is the LOE Hedge — and Its Adoption Trajectory Determines Merck's Post-2028 Floor

The bet: If Merck successfully transitions 30-40% of Keytruda patients to QLEX before IV biosimilar entry in 2028-2029, the franchise retains a $10B+ protected revenue base for 3-5 additional years. If QLEX adoption stalls at <15%, the standard 70-80% biosimilar erosion pattern plays out and Keytruda revenue collapses to ~$6-8B by 2031.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx is running the SubQ IO HCP-Patient Conversations study, which directly measures how QLEX is being positioned in real conversations. If ZoomRx can quantify the QLEX adoption inflection — especially the community vs. academic split and the impact of the April 2026 J-code — that data becomes the most valuable primary research Merck's commercial team can get.

Evidence FOR:

  • QLEX approved September 2025, launched late September — earliest possible timeline
  • Administration in ~1 minute vs 30-minute IV infusion is a meaningful convenience gain
  • Permanent J-code (J9277) effective April 1, 2026 removes the billing uncertainty that was suppressing early adoption
  • Merck projects 30-40% transition by 2028 — aggressive but not unreasonable given SubQ precedent (Herceptin SC reached ~40% in EU)
  • QLEX patent estate is separate from IV pembrolizumab patents — biosimilar manufacturers would need to develop their own SC formulation

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Q4 2025 QLEX sales were only $35M out of $8.4B total Keytruda — a <0.5% penetration rate after 3 months
  • Physician inertia with formulation switches is real — many will default to "what I've always used" until payers force it
  • IV biosimilar entry could be priced so low that even QLEX convenience doesn't justify the premium
  • Herceptin SC's success in EU has not been replicated at scale in the US market for any biologic
  • Community oncology buy-and-bill economics may actually favor IV Keytruda (higher ASP = higher margin) over SC

Confidence: Medium. The J-code inflection point (April 2026) is the first real test. If Q2 2026 QLEX revenue doesn't show meaningful acceleration, this thesis weakens significantly.

What to watch: Q2 2026 QLEX revenue. SubQ study early conversation data. Community vs academic QLEX adoption split. Payer coverage decisions for J9277.

Thesis 2: HNSCC KN-689 Will Be a Slow Burn, Not a Fast Ramp — and the Bottleneck Is Surgeons, Not Oncologists

The bet: Despite positive clinical data and strong HCP awareness (70% of ONCs and SURGs know KN-689), adoption will be slow because the decision chain runs through surgeons who historically deprioritize IO. PD-L1 testing infrastructure in HNSCC is immature, and surgeons control the MDT agenda. KN-689 will take 18-24 months to reach peak share vs. the 6-12 months typical for metastatic IO launches.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx has the most comprehensive HNSCC insight library of any MR firm — HCP-Patient conversations, biomarker testing qual, ATU baseline, listening sessions. If Vinoth can articulate this slow-ramp thesis to Merck with supporting ZoomRx data, it positions the next wave of KN-689 studies as essential tracking for a launch that requires patience and targeted intervention.

Evidence FOR:

  • ATU W1 data: Only 20-29% of Stage III-IVA patients currently receive NAT — the baseline is low
  • 53% of NSCLC conversations don't mention MDTs; HNSCC is better but 20-40% of patients still not discussed in MDT
  • PD-L1 reflexive testing is formalized in only 4/13 HNSCC practices — the rest are manual or "moving toward"
  • FNA biopsy (20-45% of patients) yields inadequate tissue for CPS testing — re-biopsy adds 1-3+ weeks
  • Community surgeon in HCP-Pt study: "not yet convinced by KN689 data; awaiting input from academic peers"
  • Surgeons control patient flow to MDT and can bypass it entirely for "straightforward" cases

Evidence AGAINST:

  • 70% self-stated KN-689 knowledge among both ONCs and SURGs — awareness is not the bottleneck
  • NCCN guideline inclusion is a powerful accelerant in oncology — many HCPs cite it as a key driver
  • Positive post-launch HCP experience: improved surgical margins, reduced burden, favorable tolerability
  • HNSCC is a smaller, more concentrated prescriber base than NSCLC — easier to activate
  • 47% of SURGs (vs. 29% ONCs) mention KEYTRUDA unprompted for LA HNSCC — surgeons may be more engaged than expected

Confidence: Medium-high. The ATU W2 (post-launch, Aug 2025 fielding) will be the first real adoption measurement. If NAT rates jump >10 percentage points from baseline, this thesis weakens.

What to watch: ATU W2 results. KN-689 listening session trends (are more surgeons recording conversations?). Reflexive PD-L1 testing adoption rates. NCCN guideline update specifics.

Thesis 3: Merck's Perioperative Strategy Is the Right Long-Term Bet — It Expands the TAM Before Biosimilars Shrink It

The bet: By moving Keytruda into neoadjuvant/adjuvant settings across multiple tumor types (NSCLC via KN-671, HNSCC via KN-689, urothelial via EV-302/KN-A39, melanoma), Merck is deliberately expanding the total addressable market by 30-50% per indication. This is the counter to the LOE narrative — even if biosimilars take 70% of the IV metastatic market, the perioperative market is newer, clinician-stickier, and less susceptible to formulary switching.

Why this matters for Vinoth: Every perioperative launch creates a ZoomRx research opportunity — HCP-Pt conversations in pre-surgical settings, referral pathway studies, biomarker testing research, and surgeon engagement tracking. If Vinoth can articulate the cross-indication perioperative thesis, he can pitch a portfolio of studies rather than one-off projects.

Evidence FOR:

  • KN-671 (NSCLC perioperative) approved March 2024 — early-stage cancers cited as a key Keytruda growth driver in 2025
  • KN-689 (HNSCC perioperative) approved June 2025 — first IO in this setting
  • Padcev+Keytruda approved perioperatively for cisplatin-ineligible MIBC — expanding into bladder
  • Perioperative patients are treatment-naive, engaged, and have longer treatment duration (neoadjuvant + surgery + adjuvant) than metastatic
  • Perioperative settings are harder for biosimilars to penetrate — physicians don't switch curative-intent regimens for cost savings

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Perioperative patient populations are smaller than metastatic per indication
  • Higher bar for benefit/risk — AEs in surgical candidates are closely scrutinized
  • Perioperative Keytruda still uses the same pembrolizumab molecule — IV biosimilars could theoretically be used perioperatively too
  • The referral chain (surgeon → MDT → oncologist → back to surgeon) creates friction that slows adoption
  • NSCLC HCP-Pt study shows 27% of conversations mention urgency to treat surgically — physician bias toward immediate surgery persists

Confidence: High. This is Merck's stated corporate strategy, backed by multiple Phase 3 readouts. The execution risk is in adoption speed, not in the strategic logic.

What to watch: Cross-indication perioperative revenue as a % of total Keytruda. ATU tracking across KN-671 and KN-689. Whether NCCN guidelines elevate perioperative IO to "preferred" from "recommended."

Thesis 4: The MDT Is the Point of Intervention — Whoever Owns the MDT Workflow Owns KN-689 Adoption

The bet: KN-689 adoption is not a traditional marketing challenge (awareness → trial → adoption). It's a workflow challenge. The MDT meeting is the decision node where PD-L1 results converge with surgical planning and treatment selection. If PD-L1 results are available at the MDT, KN-689 gets discussed. If not, it doesn't. The lever for Merck is not more rep visits — it's MDT workflow integration.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx's biomarker testing qual and HCP-Pt conversation data provide the most granular view of MDT dynamics available. If ZoomRx can map the MDT workflow by institution type (academic vs. community, reflexive vs. manual testing) and identify the specific failure points, that's a consulting deliverable that goes beyond traditional MR.

Evidence FOR:

  • Nearly all Stage III-IVA HNSCC cases are discussed in MDT, but 20-40% are not
  • When PD-L1 results are not available at MDT, the team aligns on a "conditional plan" — which often defaults to surgery
  • Surgeons present cases but rely on Med Oncs to suggest PD-L1 testing — if Med Onc isn't proactive, testing doesn't happen
  • Biomarker testing qual: "If the surgeon does not order PD-L1 at diagnosis/staging, the MDT will be the final line of defense"
  • Process barriers (delayed triggers, lack of HCP accountability) are more common than prohibitive barriers

Evidence AGAINST:

  • MDT dynamics vary enormously by institution — no single intervention works everywhere
  • Community settings often lack formal MDTs entirely
  • Surgeons who prefer immediate surgery can bypass MDT for "straightforward" cases
  • MDT workflow is not something Merck's field force can directly influence — it requires institutional change

Confidence: Medium-high. This thesis is well-supported by qualitative data but hasn't been validated quantitatively. The ATU W2 data (post-launch) could show whether MDT dynamics actually predict KN-689 uptake.

What to watch: Correlation between reflexive PD-L1 testing and KN-689 prescription rates. Surgeon-initiated vs. Onc-initiated testing patterns. Whether Merck invests in MDT-level interventions (e.g., decision support tools, pathway protocols).

Thesis 5: Biosimilar Defense Is Not One Strategy — It's Three, and They Must Work Simultaneously

The bet: Merck's Keytruda LOE defense requires three strategies working in concert: (1) QLEX product hop to move patients to a patent-protected formulation, (2) perioperative TAM expansion to create biosimilar-resistant revenue streams, and (3) indication-specific patent litigation to delay biosimilar entry in high-value indications. If any one of these fails, the others cannot compensate.

Why this matters for Vinoth: ZoomRx touches all three vectors. SubQ IO study measures QLEX adoption (Strategy 1). KN-689 and perioperative studies measure TAM expansion (Strategy 2). PET and SFEA studies measure brand loyalty metrics that predict switching vulnerability (relevant to Strategy 3 in predicting which indications are most at risk). Vinoth can position ZoomRx as the primary research partner for Merck's LOE defense — not just for one brand team, but across the franchise.

Evidence FOR:

  • Merck has 50+ active US patents covering methods-of-use, formulations, and dosing — some extending to 2042
  • QLEX has separate patent protection from IV pembrolizumab
  • Perioperative Keytruda creates treatment-naive patient cohorts less susceptible to switching
  • IRA price negotiation (Keytruda selected) creates urgency — biosimilars become even more attractive to payers if Keytruda price drops

Evidence AGAINST:

  • Patent thickets have been challenged successfully before (see Humira — AbbVie settled with 10+ biosimilar manufacturers)
  • QLEX adoption at <0.5% after 3 months is not encouraging
  • Payers may mandate IV biosimilar use regardless of QLEX availability
  • Biosimilar manufacturers are well-funded and experienced in oncology (Coherus, Samsung Bioepis, Biocon)
  • No precedent for a $30B+ biologic successfully defending against biosimilar erosion

Confidence: Medium. This is the most uncertain thesis because it depends on legal, regulatory, and market dynamics that are outside ZoomRx's measurement domain. But the ZoomRx-measurable components (QLEX adoption, perioperative loyalty, brand switching propensity) are still highly valuable to Merck.

What to watch: Biosimilar litigation outcomes. QLEX quarterly revenue trajectory. Payer formulary decisions regarding QLEX vs. IV Keytruda vs. future biosimilars. CMS price negotiation outcome for Keytruda.

Cross-Thesis Summary

Thesis Confidence Next Evidence Point Vinoth's Action
1. QLEX adoption determines post-2028 floor Medium Q2 2026 QLEX revenue Track SubQ study recruitment; push for J-code impact analysis
2. KN-689 slow burn — surgeons are bottleneck Medium-high ATU W2 (Aug 2025) Use listening session trends to validate; pitch surgeon-specific research
3. Perioperative TAM expansion is the right bet High Cross-indication perioperative revenue Pitch portfolio perioperative tracking study to Merck
4. MDT is the intervention point Medium-high ATU W2 correlation analysis Package biomarker + MDT workflow findings as consulting deliverable
5. Three-pronged LOE defense must work together Medium Biosimilar litigation; QLEX revenue Position ZoomRx as LOE defense research partner

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
